text: The surgeon uses a liver retractor to elevate the liver, exposing the upper stomach. The gallbladder appears normal, and the hiatus is visually assessed as normal without signs of a hiatal hernia.
The stomach is visible in the foreground, partially covered by the omentum, which will be uncovered as the procedure progresses.
The surgeon places robotic instruments, including a grasper and a vessel sealing device visible on the right side of the screen, in preparation for the gastrectomy procedure.
The robotic vessel sealing device is used to cauterize small blood vessels in a focused area. Once cauterization is confirmed, a blade within the device's jaws cuts the tissue.

The surgeon manipulates the robotic instruments to work on the greater curve of the stomach, accessing the free space behind it. Blood vessels within the fatty tissue are divided as the tool changes direction from proximal to downward along the stomach's curvature.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to divide blood vessels and mobilize the stomach, moving distally towards the pylorus, which is located to the left but not visible in the video.
The surgeon mobilizes the greater curve of the stomach, moving proximally, while taking several bites along the greater curve. The fat pad is momentarily moved out of the way to improve visibility.

The robotic instruments are used to move spanning tissue, uncovering the upper part of the stomach. The surgeon uses the vessel sealer to mobilize the greater curve of the stomach, maneuvering around the fundus and working towards the spleen, carefully managing the difficult fat at the top of the stomach.

The spleen comes into view as the surgeon uses the robotic vessel sealer to carefully navigate the delicate area where the stomach is closely attached to the spleen. The robotic arm slides underneath to expose the high posterior short gastric vessels, referred to as the 'pocket.'

The surgeon uses the vessel sealer to delicately dissect the pocket area, carefully mobilizing the stomach away from the spleen to avoid excessive traction. The diaphragm and hiatus begin to come into view as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon completes the dissection near the spleen, bringing the hiatus into view. The anterior border of the left cruce is visible as tissue is cleared from the front side of the hiatus. The base is cleared to prevent any redundant stomach pocket. The greater curve of the stomach is fully mobilized, and the stomach is repositioned in its normal anatomical orientation, preparing for the stapling process.

The surgical assistant uses an Ethicon stapler to perform the first staple firing at the bottom end of the stomach. The surgeon then turns the corner at the incisura, ensuring adequate space to prevent folding or kinking. A 34 French bougie sizing device is advanced into the stomach, visible as it descends, to maintain a uniform sleeve during the stapling process.

The surgeon advances the stapler higher on the stomach, moving closer to the visible bougie sizing device inside the stomach. Using robotic tools, the stomach is positioned to ensure a uniform sleeve. After each staple firing, the surgeon inspects the crotch of the staple line for any leftover staples, ensuring the sleeve is narrow and unconstricted near the top of the stomach.

The surgeon examines the posterior aspect of the stomach to ensure exclusion of a large pocket, then fires the stapler to separate and seal the stomach, leaving a small bridge of tissue. The stapler is positioned to divide this remaining bridge using a load suitable for thinner tissue near the diaphragm, completing the formation of the gastric sleeve. The excess stomach tissue is set aside for removal, and preparations for a leak test begin.

The surgeon uses a device to occlude the flow from the stomach while the anesthesia doctor advances a nasogastric tube into the stomach. The stomach is inflated under substantial pressure with methylene blue dye, visibly expanding. The robotic arm is used to assess the pressure within the stomach. The surgeon inspects the staple line for any blue dye leakage, indicating potential leaks, and evaluates the shape of the sleeve to ensure it forms a uniform tube without constrictions.

The surgeon inspects the cut edge of the staple line for bleeding, observing slight oozing at the site of the first staple firing where the tissue is thicker. Focused, controlled cautery is applied to the area to stop the bleeding. The remainder of the staple line is checked and found to be free of bleeding points.

The surgeon uses the robotic needle driver to place a figure of eight suture around a segment of fatty tissue containing large trunk blood vessels. The suture encircles and pinches off the vessels to prevent clot propagation into the main vein system of the abdomen. The robotic system facilitates precise needle placement and suture tying.

The surgeon uses a silk suture to attach divided tissue to the staple line, aiming to prevent postoperative twisting of the sleeve. The suture is placed with precision to stabilize the sleeve structure.

The surgeon removes the liver retractor and relocates the camera to a different position on the abdomen, viewing from the patient's left to right side. A red spot indicates a previous trocar site, while the purplish mobilized stomach tissue is visible in the foreground. Using a large grasper, the surgeon spreads the muscle and pulls the stomach through a small incision. The greater curvature of the stomach is extracted, and the muscle is closed with a suture using the Carter-Thompson Suture Passer. Numbing medicine is infiltrated into the tissue to minimize postoperative pain.
